_cite.reason: URL-normalization 1c: strip trailing markdown-emphasis `*`/backtick glued to citation URLs in intelligence_item_sections.content_md (customer-checkable link + validate_item_provenance criterion-2 exact-compare). Operator ruling 2026-07-04. Same transform as the write-site fix.

prior.content_md:
The Estonian Climate Ministry (Kliimaministeerium, operating under the legacy domain envir.ee) is the competent authority responsible for energy policy, environmental protection, transport infrastructure, maritime affairs, and circular economy in Estonia. It sits at the apex of the national regulatory supply chain for all initiatives covered by this intelligence item.

For the workspace — a freight forwarder operating across air, road, and ocean modes with Estonian and Baltic trade lane exposure — the relevant chain positions are as follows:

**Energy and fuel supply chain:** Estonia's Energy Sector Development Plan 2035 (ENMAK 2035) sets the overarching framework for electricity generation, fuel market liberalisation, and renewable energy targets. The workspace is positioned as an *end-user* (commercial consumer) of electricity and fuel within this chain. It does not hold any producer, grid operator, or support-scheme participant role. However, it is directly affected by pricing outcomes: the ministry projects electricity prices falling from 9 cents/kWh to 6.6 cents/kWh by 2030 as reverse-auction-procured wind capacity comes online.

**Wind bonus/municipality support chain:** Under the Wind Speed Bonus programme, the primary regulated actors are wind energy developers and municipalities. The workspace is a *downstream beneficiary* — indirectly — through lower future energy tariffs, and has no registration or reporting obligations under this programme.

**ASF/wildlife management chain:** The workspace occupies a *regulated transport operator* position under veterinary and biosecurity rules enforced by the Estonian Agriculture and Food Board (PTA) and, at EU level, under Regulation (EU) 2020/687 (animal disease control). Road freight vehicles transiting ASF-restricted zones are subject to mandatory disinfection and movement-restriction rules. The workspace must treat this as an active compliance obligation, not merely a monitoring matter.

**Marine/EEZ chain:** Plans to establish marine protected areas (MPAs) in Estonia's EEZ (approximately 11,421 km²) are relevant to ocean freight operators routing through the Baltic Sea. The workspace's ocean mode is secondary, but routes through the Gulf of Finland, Northern Baltic Proper, and Gulf of Riga may be subject to navigation restrictions once MPAs are formally designated. Legal confirmation of the workspace's status as a *vessel operator or charterer* versus *cargo owner* will determine the precise compliance burden; this role placement requires legal review.

**Recovery and Resilience Plan:** Estonia's updated RRP (February 2026) channels EU funds through national agencies. The workspace has no direct registration obligation but benefits from grid access expansion investments that affect third-party logistics facility energy costs.

*Source: Ministry of Climate of Estonia (Kliimaministeerium), About the Ministry, https://kliimaministeerium.ee/en/ministry-news-and-contact/about-ministry; Energy Sector Development Plan 2035, https://kliimaministeerium.ee/en/energy-sector-development-plan; Estonian Exclusive Economic Zone Act, Riigi Teataja, https://www.riigiteataja.ee/en/eli/ee/520012015003/consolide.*
